behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: local module wiring (filesystem discovery + filename parsing/sorting + runner execution order). The failure is not only sort logic; it can occur at the handoff where discovered files are executed unsorted. Pass 2 mapping: Integration is the minimum because a temp migrations directory plus wired runner catches real ordering failures. Rejected higher level: behavior text only requires order by filename prefix; it does not require real subprocess/OS timing/network effects, real model output, or multi-agent steps.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp migrations directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., 010_b.sql, 002_a.sql, 020_c.sql). Run the runner with an instrumented migration-applier/repo boundary that records execution sequence. Assert the applied order is 002, 010, 020 and metadata records follow that same order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: module interaction with persisted local DB state (_migrations table read/write across runs). A unit test of a single filter function misses failures where repository state is read incorrectly or recorded inconsistently. Pass 2 mapping: Integration with a real local test DB schema is the minimum. Rejected higher level: behavior text says 'subsequent run' idempotency, but does not require separate processes, OS/network fault behavior, or AI/ML output.
behaviors.test_description: Against a local test PostgreSQL database, run migrations once and capture side effects. Run the runner again on the same database. Assert no migration SQL is executed the second time and _migrations contains one record per file with no duplicates.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: transaction + control-flow wiring across runner, DB transaction boundary, and migration loop. Mocking transaction behavior can hide rollback defects, so local DB-backed integration is needed. Pass 2 mapping: Integration is minimum because one process with local DB can expose rollback/stop-on-failure semantics. Rejected higher level: behavior text is about single-run failure handling, not subprocess orchestration or OS/network behavior; no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare three migrations where the second intentionally fails after making a write in the same transaction. Execute runner once. Assert writes from the failing migration are absent (rolled back), third migration was not attempted, and _migrations includes only migrations completed before the failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: real concurrent processes + shared DB locking/coordination behavior. This failure appears at process/DB boundary and interleaving timing; in-process mocks often serialize and miss races. Pass 2 mapping: System is minimum because the behavior text explicitly requires 'two instances ... at the same time ... from separate processes.' Rejected lower level: Integration is too low because it may not reproduce real process concurrency and lock contention.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two OS processes of the migration runner simultaneously against the same PostgreSQL instance and same migrations directory. After both exit, assert each migration’s data side effect appears exactly once and _migrations has exactly one row per migration (no duplicates, no missing entries).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Pass 1 dependency type: initialization sequencing across modules (metadata bootstrap then migration apply) with local DB state. A unit test of table-name constants or SQL strings cannot catch ordering defects between bootstrap and executor. Pass 2 mapping: Integration is minimum using a fresh local DB. Rejected higher level: behavior text is single-process first-run ordering; it does not require subprocess race conditions, OS/network behavior, or AI/ML output.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a brand-new empty test PostgreSQL database and at least one migration file that depends on metadata writes. Run runner once and verify _migrations exists before first migration record insertion/apply step (e.g., via query checkpoints/logged SQL order), and that run completes with expected metadata entries.
